questions.stem: According to the deck's working definition (Ford, Parsons & Kua), technical debt is best described as which of the following?

options.text: Stuff that isn't supposed to be there and is in the way of the stuff that is supposed to be there
Any code that lacks unit tests and continuous integration
The total number of static-analysis violations reported by a tool
Code that was written more than five years ago by former employees

questions.explanation: The deck quotes Building Evolutionary Architectures (p.110): technical debt is 'stuff that isn't supposed to be there and is in the way of the stuff that is supposed to be there.' B describes a CI/test gap, not debt; C is a snapshot metric the deck criticises; D conflates debt with age, which the deck explicitly rejects. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.2)

questions.stem: Why does the deck emphasise the phrase 'is in the way' in its technical-debt definition?

options.text: Because debt is defined purely by the absolute amount of code present
Because being 'in the way' means the code physically blocks the build pipeline
Because clutter that nobody touches charges no real cost, so only obstructive code counts as debt that matters
Because all clutter is equally costly regardless of whether it is touched

questions.explanation: The guide argues that dormant clutter is harmless; debt only 'costs' when it obstructs the work you need to do, which is why where the debt sits matters more than how much there is. D contradicts the emphasis; A reduces debt to volume; B misreads 'in the way' literally. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.2, p.12)

questions.stem: The technical-debt metaphor was originally coined by which person?

options.text: Adam Tornhill
Rich Hickey
Manny Lehman
Ward Cunningham

questions.explanation: The guide notes the metaphor itself is originally Ward Cunningham's, framing substandard-but-shipped code as a financial loan. Tornhill built CodeScene; Hickey gave the 'complexity kills' talk; Lehman gave the laws of evolution. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.2)

questions.stem: Which source does the deck cite for its working definition of technical debt?

options.text: Martin, Clean Code
Ford, Parsons & Kua, Building Evolutionary Architectures (p.110)
Fowler, Refactoring
Graves et al., Predicting Fault Incidence Using Software Change History

questions.explanation: The definition 'stuff that isn't supposed to be there and is in the way...' is attributed to Ford, Parsons & Kua's Building Evolutionary Architectures p.110. Fowler/Martin supply refactoring and clean-code material; Graves et al. is the fault-prediction study. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.2)

questions.stem: A team has a 40,000-line module of extremely tangled code that no one has modified in three years and that is unlikely to ever change again. Per the deck's definition, how should this be classified?

options.text: The single highest-priority hotspot because of its size
Not debt at all, since untouched code can never be substandard
Legacy code that must be refactored immediately to reduce principal
Dormant debt that charges no interest, because it is not 'in the way' of current work

questions.explanation: Because the module is never touched, it is not in the way of current work, so it charges no interest and is dormant debt. D is correct: complexity alone is principal, but cost is principal times interest. A reads size as priority (wrong axis); B ignores that the code can still be substandard; C urges action the economics do not justify. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.2, p.12)

questions.stem: Which two of Lehman's Laws of software evolution does the deck use to ground technical debt?

options.text: Continuing Change and Increasing Complexity
Conservation of Familiarity and Conservation of Organisational Stability
Self-Regulation and Continuing Growth
Declining Quality and Feedback System

questions.explanation: The deck grounds debt in Lehman's Continuing Change and Increasing Complexity. The other options name real Lehman laws not invoked by this deck, so they are plausible but not what the slide uses. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.3)

questions.stem: Lehman's law of Continuing Change states which of the following?

options.text: A system's functional content must continually increase to maintain user satisfaction
The size of a system tends to remain statistically self-regulating
A system must be continually adapted or it becomes progressively less satisfactory
As a system evolves, its complexity increases unless work is done to maintain or reduce it

questions.explanation: Continuing Change: a system must be continually adapted or it becomes progressively less satisfactory. D is the Increasing Complexity law; A and B paraphrase other Lehman laws not used here. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.3)

questions.stem: Lehman's law of Increasing Complexity states which of the following?

options.text: A system must be continually adapted or it becomes progressively less satisfactory
Complexity always decreases automatically as a system matures
Complexity is fixed at design time and cannot change
As a system evolves, its complexity increases unless work is done to maintain or reduce it

questions.explanation: Increasing Complexity: as a system evolves, its complexity rises unless work is done to maintain or reduce it. D is correct; A is the Continuing Change law; B and C both deny that complexity creep is the default trajectory. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.3)

questions.stem: Why do Lehman's two laws together make technical debt 'structural rather than accidental'?

options.text: You are required to keep changing the system, and every change tends to raise complexity, so debt accumulates unless you actively counter it
Because Lehman proved debt is impossible to remove once incurred
Because the laws forbid any refactoring during evolution
Because complexity only grows when developers make mistakes

questions.explanation: Continuing Change forces ongoing modification while Increasing Complexity means each change tends to raise complexity, so debt accumulates as a default unless effort counters it; that is what makes it structural. B overstates (debt is removable via refactoring); C is false; D blames mistakes rather than the structural trajectory. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.3)

questions.stem: How does the pairing of Lehman's laws justify treating refactoring as a permanent, recurring activity rather than a one-off cleanup?

options.text: Because the system must keep changing (Law 1) and each change tends to add complexity (Law 2), so countering effort must be continuous too
Because Lehman's laws explicitly mandate a quarterly refactoring sprint
Because one large refactoring removes all future complexity permanently
Because the laws apply only to systems that have stopped evolving

questions.explanation: Continuing Change keeps generating modifications and Increasing Complexity adds principal with each one, so the counter-effort (refactoring) must be ongoing, not a one-time event. B invents a schedule; C is false (new changes re-add complexity); D inverts the laws' scope. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.3)

questions.stem: A manager argues that the team should refactor the whole codebase once and then never need to again. Using Lehman's laws, what is the best rebuttal?

options.text: Complexity is constant, so one cleanup is indeed sufficient
Continuing Change means future modifications keep arriving, and Increasing Complexity means each re-adds principal, so cleanup must recur
Lehman's laws prove a single cleanup is mathematically optimal
Refactoring is forbidden by Lehman's laws, so the team should not do it at all

questions.explanation: Because the system must keep changing and each change tends to raise complexity, a one-off cleanup will be eroded by subsequent changes, so refactoring must be recurring. C and A contradict Increasing Complexity; D misstates the implication of the laws. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.3)

questions.stem: Which speaker does the deck quote for the claim that ignoring complexity will 'eventually kill you' and make every sprint accomplish less?

options.text: Ward Cunningham
Martin Fowler
Rich Hickey (Simple Made Easy)
Adam Tornhill (Your Code as a Crime Scene)

questions.explanation: Slide 4 quotes Rich Hickey's Simple Made Easy on complexity killing velocity. Tornhill is the CodeScene author; Cunningham coined the debt metaphor; Fowler wrote Refactoring. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.4)

questions.stem: What is the central economic point of the Hickey slide on complexity?

options.text: Complexity only affects code readability, not delivery speed
Simple code is always slower to write initially than complex code, so debt is unavoidable
Complexity has no measurable effect until a full rewrite is needed
Technical debt is a velocity and economics problem, not just an aesthetic one, because unmanaged complexity compounds and each sprint delivers less

questions.explanation: The slide reframes debt as a velocity/economics problem: ignored complexity compounds so each sprint accomplishes less. A and C understate the impact; B inverts the long-run argument (simple sustains throughput). (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.4)

questions.stem: The speed-versus-time chart on the Hickey slide contrasts 'Easy' and 'Simple' code. What does it show?

options.text: The easy path sustains the highest speed over the long haul
The easy/complex path starts fast but its speed collapses, while the simple path sustains throughput
The simple path is always slower at every point in time
Both paths converge to the same speed eventually

questions.explanation: The chart plots speed against time: easy/complex starts fast then collapses, while simple sustains throughput over the long haul. C, D and A all misdescribe the divergence the chart illustrates. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.4)

questions.stem: How does the Hickey slide reframe spending on refactoring?

options.text: As a purely aesthetic activity with no economic justification
As something to do only after a product has been retired
As an investment in future delivery speed rather than a pure cost
As an unavoidable tax that produces no return

questions.explanation: Because unmanaged complexity compounds and slows every sprint, the slide reframes refactoring spend as an investment in future delivery speed, not a cost. D, A and B all deny the investment framing the slide establishes. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.4)

questions.stem: On the slide showing technical debt's impact, which two symptoms are listed on the business/roadmap side?

options.text: Long lead times and lack of predictability
Bugs and crashes users experience
High duplication and low test coverage
Large file sizes and deep nesting

questions.explanation: The business/roadmap side shows long lead times and lack of predictability. Bugs are the product side (B); C and D are static-analysis style metrics, not the business symptoms shown. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.5)

questions.stem: On the technical-debt impact slide, what does the product side (what users experience) represent?

options.text: Lack of predictability
High duplication percentage
Bugs
Long lead times

questions.explanation: The product side, drawn as a swarm of bugs/beetles, represents the bugs users experience. Long lead times and lack of predictability are on the business side; duplication is a static metric. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.5)

questions.stem: What is the teaching point of the slide that pays a 'BILL' for technical debt to both a business side and a product side?

options.text: Technical debt only affects developers and never reaches the business
The business and product impacts are identical and interchangeable
Paying the bill once eliminates debt permanently
Technical debt is a business liability, not just a developer inconvenience, because it manifests as slow/unpredictable delivery and as user-facing defects

questions.explanation: The slide shows debt paid out to both business (slow, unpredictable delivery) and product (bugs), making the point that debt is a business liability, not merely a developer inconvenience. A denies the outward impact; B ignores the two distinct sides; C misreads the metaphor. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.5)

questions.stem: A stakeholder dismisses technical debt as 'just a developer concern.' Using the impact slide, what is the strongest counter-argument?

options.text: Debt affects only the speed of writing new code, never the product
Debt manifests outward as long lead times and unpredictable delivery to the business and as bugs to users, so it is a business liability worth prioritising
Debt is invisible to anyone outside the engineering team
Debt only matters once a static analyser reports more than 10% debt

questions.explanation: The slide makes debt's cost concrete on both sides: long lead times and unpredictability (business) and bugs (product), so it is a measurable business liability. C contradicts the outward manifestation; D ties it to a snapshot metric the deck criticises; A ignores the product (bug) side. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.5)

questions.stem: In the deck's mapping of the financial metaphor onto code, what does the 'principal' correspond to?

options.text: The code complexity you took on (size, cyclomatic complexity, nesting)
How often the file is modified in the version-control history
The number of developers who own the file
The percentage of duplicated lines

questions.explanation: Principal = code complexity (size, cyclomatic complexity, nesting) per the hotspot slide. Change frequency is the interest rate (B); ownership and duplication are different dimensions. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.12)

questions.stem: In the deck's mapping of the financial metaphor onto code, what does the 'interest rate' correspond to?

options.text: The number of static-analysis violations in the file
The code change frequency — how often the file is modified in the VCS history
The cyclomatic complexity of the file
The total number of lines in the file

questions.explanation: Interest rate = change frequency (how often the file is modified in version control). Cyclomatic complexity and LOC are the principal/complexity axis; violations are a snapshot metric. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.12)

questions.stem: Which statement captures the 'crucial insight' the deck draws from the principal/interest mapping?

options.text: Principal and interest are the same measurement under different names
The cost is the sum of principal and interest, added together
Principal alone is not the cost; the actual ongoing cost is principal times interest rate — complex code you keep changing
Interest rate alone determines the cost regardless of complexity

questions.explanation: The insight is that complexity alone (principal) charges no cost if never touched; the ongoing cost is principal times interest (complex code you keep changing). D ignores complexity; A conflates the two distinct axes; B uses addition where the deck uses a product. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.12)

questions.stem: File X has very high complexity but is changed once a year; file Y has moderate complexity but is changed weekly. Using the principal/interest model, which is paying more interest?

options.text: Neither, because interest applies only to files with zero owners
File Y, because high change frequency is the interest rate, so its moderate principal is charged often
File X, because its high complexity (principal) automatically means high interest
Both pay identical interest, since interest depends only on complexity

questions.explanation: Interest is driven by change frequency; Y's weekly changes charge its principal repeatedly, so it pays more interest despite lower complexity. C confuses principal with interest; D wrongly ties interest to complexity; A invents an ownership condition. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.12)

questions.stem: How is the principal axis ('code complexity') visualised in CodeScene's circle diagrams?

options.text: By the chord arcs connecting circles
By the vertical position of the circle
By circle size — bigger circles mean more complex/larger code
By circle colour intensity (red)

questions.explanation: Complexity (principal) is shown by circle size; redness shows change frequency (interest). Chord arcs depict change coupling, and position is just packing. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.12, p.13)

questions.stem: How is the interest-rate axis ('change frequency') visualised in CodeScene's circle diagrams?

options.text: By circle size
By the number of nested folders
By the thickness of the circle's outline
By colour intensity — redder circles mean more frequent change

questions.explanation: Change frequency (interest) is shown by red colour intensity; circle size shows complexity (principal). The other options are not how the two axes are encoded. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.12, p.13)

questions.stem: The deck defines a hotspot as which of the following?

options.text: Complicated code that you have to work with often
The file with the most lines of code in the repository
Any file flagged by a static analyser
Code written by a developer who has left the company

questions.explanation: The deck's definition of a hotspot is 'complicated code that you have to work with often.' B is size alone; C is a snapshot flag; D describes abandoned/legacy code, a separate concept. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.12)

questions.stem: Formally, a hotspot is the conjunction of which two properties?

options.text: Large file size and a single owner
Low test coverage and high cyclomatic complexity
High code complexity (principal) and high change frequency (interest rate)
High duplication and low comment density

questions.explanation: A hotspot is high complexity (principal) AND high change frequency (interest). D, A and B mix in metrics that are not the two defining hotspot axes. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.12)

questions.stem: Hotspots are the deck's answer to which question?

options.text: Where do you start when you want to pay back the debt?
How many total violations does the codebase contain?
Which programming language has the least debt?
How many developers should be assigned to each module?

questions.explanation: Hotspots answer the Tower-of-Babel question 'where do you start paying back?' by pointing to complex, frequently-changed code. B is the snapshot question; C and D are unrelated. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.8, p.12)

questions.stem: What kind of diagram does CodeScene use to visualise hotspots across a codebase?

options.text: A UML class diagram
A nested-circle enclosure (circle-packing) diagram
A Gantt chart of release dates
A flat bar chart of violation counts

questions.explanation: CodeScene renders the codebase as a nested-circle enclosure (circle-packing) diagram: folders nest as pale circles, files are inner circles, size = complexity, red = change frequency. The other diagram types are not used for hotspot maps. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.13)

questions.stem: What is the stated advantage of a hotspot map over a static violation report?

options.text: It guarantees the code will compile after refactoring
You instantly see the handful of files worth attention instead of a flat list of thousands of violations
It reports a single precise debt percentage for budgeting
It lists every violation by severity in a sortable table

questions.explanation: The hotspot map's advantage is that it surfaces the handful of files worth attention rather than a flat list of thousands of violations. C and D describe the snapshot tools the deck critiques; A is unrelated to the visualisation. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.13)

questions.stem: A located concept for a change request lands inside a file that CodeScene marks as a hotspot. What does the change-process link in the guide advise?

options.text: Ignore it, since hotspots only matter for documentation
Refactor every other file first and leave the hotspot untouched
Treat it as a red flag: budget extra prefactoring and expect higher regression risk during verification
Skip impact analysis because hotspots are already well understood

questions.explanation: A hotspot in the impact set signals expensive, frequently-churned, complex code, so the guide advises extra prefactoring and heightened regression risk during verification. D, A and B all neglect the elevated risk and cost the hotspot implies. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.12)

questions.stem: In a circle map, a small dark-red circle and a large pale circle appear. Which is more likely the priority hotspot?

options.text: The large pale circle, because size means more complexity
Neither, because only colour matters and red means low priority
Both equally, because size and colour are the same axis
The small dark-red circle, because red marks high change frequency (high interest)

questions.explanation: Redness encodes change frequency (interest), so a small dark-red circle is frequently churned and likely the priority; a large pale circle is complex but rarely changed (dormant principal). A reads size as priority; B inverts the colour meaning; C conflates the two distinct axes. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.13)

questions.stem: How does the deck characterise conventional static-analysis output (e.g. a SonarQube dashboard)?

options.text: As a behavioural analysis of how the team works with the code
As a snapshot — a point-in-time report that tells you how much debt exists but not where to start
As a movie that adds time and people dimensions
As a predictive ML model of future faults

questions.explanation: Conventional static analysis is characterised as a snapshot: precise about how much debt exists but silent on where to start. The 'movie', time/people dimensions, and behavioural analysis are CodeScene's contributions, not static analysis. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.6-9)

questions.stem: How does the deck characterise CodeScene's behavioural/evolutionary analysis?

options.text: As a flat list of static violations
As a UML reverse-engineering tool
As a movie rather than a snapshot
As a single global debt percentage

questions.explanation: CodeScene is described as a 'movie rather than a snapshot' because it mines the VCS history over time. D and A describe the snapshot tools; B is unrelated. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.9)

questions.stem: What two dimensions does the deck say behavioural analysis adds that conventional static tools miss?

options.text: Cyclomatic complexity and duplication
Lines of code and comment density
Severity and rule category
The time aspect and organization & people

questions.explanation: CodeScene adds the time aspect (evolution over VCS history) and organization & people (who works on the code). The other options are static metrics that snapshot tools already report. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.10)

questions.stem: The Sonar-style dashboard on the 'Actionable?' slide reports a headline figure. What was it?

options.text: Technical Debt: 11.0% / EUR 341,563 / 683 man days
Technical Debt: 50% / EUR 1,000,000 / 2,000 man days
Technical Debt: 0% / fully clean
Technical Debt: 25% / 100 man days

questions.explanation: The dashboard's headline was Technical Debt 11.0% / EUR 341,563 / 683 man days. The other figures do not match the slide and are distractors. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.7)

questions.stem: What does the 'Thousands of Years of Technical Debt' (Tower of Babel) slide dramatise?

options.text: That debt can be fully repaid in a single sprint
That older code always has fewer faults than new code
That static analysis is the only way to find debt
That systems accumulate so much debt over time that the hard problem becomes knowing where to start paying it back

questions.explanation: The Tower-of-Babel slide dramatises age and accretion: so much debt accumulates that the real problem is where to start. A and B are false; C contradicts the deck's whole behavioural argument. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.8)

questions.stem: Which statement best captures the deck's claim about the limits of static analysis?

options.text: Static analysis will never be able to tell you if excess code complexity actually matters
Static analysis can perfectly rank which debt to repay first
Static analysis is the only reliable way to measure change frequency
Static analysis already incorporates the time and people dimensions

questions.explanation: The deck states static analysis can never tell you if excess complexity actually matters — you need the change-frequency dimension. B, C and D overstate static analysis or attribute behavioural capabilities to it. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.10)

questions.stem: What are the three input sources in CodeScene's analysis pipeline?

options.text: Source code, CPU profiles, and memory dumps
Source code, version-control data (git), and project-management tools (e.g. JIRA)
Source code, unit tests, and the production database
Compiler warnings, linter output, and code review comments

questions.explanation: The pipeline inputs are source code + version-control data (git) + project-management tools such as JIRA. The other options list inputs the pipeline does not use. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.11)

questions.stem: In CodeScene's pipeline, what sits between the metrics stage and the final visualisations/priorities?

options.text: Pattern detectors, machine learning and intelligence
A SonarQube static rule engine
A manual spreadsheet review
A UML class-diagram generator

questions.explanation: The pipeline runs inputs into Code/Process/Evolutionary metrics, then pattern detectors / ML / intelligence, then visualisations, priorities and predictive analytics. B is a snapshot tool; C and D are not part of the pipeline. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.11)

questions.stem: What three categories of metrics does CodeScene derive from its inputs?

options.text: Coverage, mutation, and branch metrics
Code, process, and evolutionary metrics
Static, dynamic, and runtime metrics
Security, performance, and accessibility metrics

questions.explanation: CodeScene derives Code, Process and Evolutionary metrics. The other triples are unrelated metric families not named in the pipeline. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.11)

questions.stem: Summarised in one line, what is the deck's contrast between static and behavioural analysis?

options.text: Both measure exactly the same thing with different visuals
Static analysis is predictive; behavioural analysis is point-in-time
Static analysis measures the code; behavioural analysis measures how people work with the code
Static analysis measures people; behavioural analysis measures the code

questions.explanation: The one-line contrast: static analysis measures the code, while behavioural analysis measures how people work with the code (and that reveals which debt costs interest). D inverts it; A denies the distinction; B swaps which one is the snapshot. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.10-11)

questions.stem: Approximately how many violations did the 'Actionable?' Sonar dashboard report, and why does the deck mention it?

options.text: About 1,000,000 violations — to show the tool over-reports
About 10,072 violations — to show a long flat list is precise but gives no starting point
About 50 violations — to show static analysis finds almost nothing
Zero violations — to show the codebase was already clean

questions.explanation: The dashboard reported roughly 10,072 violations (8,794 Major); the deck cites it to argue that a long flat list is precise but not actionable. The other counts do not match the slide. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.7)

questions.stem: Which research paper does the deck cite to back the hotspot heuristic with empirical evidence?

options.text: Beck, Test-Driven Development by Example
Lehman, Programs, Life Cycles, and Laws of Software Evolution
Graves et al., Predicting Fault Incidence Using Software Change History
Fowler, Refactoring: Improving the Design of Existing Code

questions.explanation: Slide 14 cites Graves, Karr, Marron & Siy, Predicting Fault Incidence Using Software Change History (2000). The other works are real but are not the fault-prediction study used here. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.14)

questions.stem: According to Graves et al. as quoted by the deck, which better predicts how many faults code will contain?

options.text: The length (size) of the code
The number of comments in the code
The number of classes in the file
The number of times the code has been changed

questions.explanation: Graves et al.: the number of times code has been changed is a better fault indicator than its length — change frequency beats size, which is the interest-rate axis. A is the metric they found inferior; B and C were not the finding. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.14)

questions.stem: In the Graves et al. model, how were a module's past changes weighted to estimate its fault potential?

options.text: Only the very first change ever made counted
Only changes older than five years counted
As a weighted sum of all past changes, with large, recent changes weighted most heavily
As a simple count of changes, all weighted equally

questions.explanation: The most successful model used a weighted sum of all past changes, weighting large, recent changes most heavily — recent churn matters most. D ignores recency/size weighting; A and B invert which changes count. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.14)

questions.stem: What does the Graves et al. finding imply about which file to prioritise for repayment?

options.text: The largest file by line count, regardless of change history
The file with the most comments
The oldest file in the repository, regardless of churn
The most-changed-recently file, not necessarily the biggest one

questions.explanation: Since change frequency (especially recent churn) predicts faults better than size, you prioritise the most-changed-recently file, not the biggest. A prioritises size; B and C use irrelevant criteria. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.14)

questions.stem: How does the Graves et al. result provide the evidential justification for the hotspot model's interest-rate axis?

options.text: It shows how often (and how recently) code changes predicts defects better than how big it is, justifying change frequency as the central prioritisation axis
It shows code size is the only reliable fault predictor, justifying complexity as the only axis
It shows faults are random and cannot be predicted, so any axis is arbitrary
It shows comment density is the strongest predictor of faults

questions.explanation: The paper's finding that change frequency (and recency) beats size for fault prediction is the empirical basis for placing change frequency at the centre of debt prioritisation. B inverts the finding; C and D contradict the study. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.14)

questions.stem: On the 'Worrisome Trends' slide, what makes the TypeScript checker.ts complexity trend a warning sign?

options.text: The complexity curve rises far faster than the nearly flat lines-of-code curve
Both complexity and lines of code fall together
The lines-of-code curve rises while complexity stays flat
Both curves remain perfectly flat over time

questions.explanation: The warning sign is complexity rising faster than size: the red complexity curve climbs steeply while the blue LOC curve stays nearly flat, meaning the file is getting harder per line. The other options describe non-worrisome or inverted trends. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.15)

questions.stem: On the checker.ts trend slide, what 'Code Health' band was the file in?

options.text: 5 — a neutral middle band
0 — meaning the file was deleted
1 — the lowest band, shown in red
10 — the highest, healthiest band

questions.explanation: checker.ts scored Code Health 1, the lowest band, shown in red. The other values misstate the score or its meaning. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.15)

questions.stem: The checker.ts slide reports Knowledge Loss 0%. What does that figure mean in CodeScene's terms?

options.text: The file has lost all of its original developers
The file has zero test coverage
The file is 0% complete
No abandoned code — all of the file is still owned by current contributors

questions.explanation: Knowledge Loss measures abandoned code (authors gone); 0% means none of checker.ts is abandoned — it is still owned. A inverts the meaning; B and C confuse it with coverage or completeness. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.15)

questions.stem: checker.ts had about 785 commits and 1,051 defects (133% bug fixes). Combined with its rising complexity trend, what is the correct interpretation?

options.text: The high commit count proves the debt is already paid down
The defect count is irrelevant once complexity is known
The debt is compounding in a high-interest, defect-prone file, so it warrants action now
The file is healthy because it has many commits

questions.explanation: High commits (interest) plus many defects plus complexity outpacing LOC means the debt is compounding in a high-interest, defect-prone file — act now. D and A misread churn/defects as health; B ignores the corroborating defect evidence. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.15)

questions.stem: What is the purpose of CodeScene's X-Ray feature?

options.text: To compile the project and report build errors
To generate a UML diagram of the whole system
To measure the runtime performance of the application
To drill inside a hotspot file and rank its individual functions by change frequency and complexity

questions.explanation: X-Ray is a deep dive that zooms inside a hotspot file to find its most problematic functions, ranking them by change frequency and cyclomatic complexity. The other options are unrelated tools/activities. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.16-18)

questions.stem: Why does the deck argue you should X-Ray a hotspot file rather than refactor the whole file?

options.text: A hotspot file can be huge (e.g. 25k LOC); X-Ray scopes the fix to the two or three worst functions
Refactoring a whole file is always faster than fixing functions
X-Ray automatically rewrites the file for you
Whole-file refactoring is forbidden by version control

questions.explanation: A 25k-LOC hotspot is not a single refactoring task; X-Ray ranks functions so you fix the two or three worst, scoping the work affordably. B is false; C overstates X-Ray (it analyses, not rewrites); D is invented. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.17)

questions.stem: In the React attach.* X-Ray table, which two functions are the worst function-level hotspots by cyclomatic complexity?

options.text: recordMount (8) and inspectElement (12)
attach top-level context (9) and flushPendingEvents (15)
handleCommitFiberRoot (19) and recordMount (8)
updateFiberRecursively (48) and inspectElementRaw (45)

questions.explanation: updateFiberRecursively (cyclomatic complexity 48) and inspectElementRaw (45) are the function-level hotspots to refactor first. The other pairs have markedly lower complexity in the table. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.18)

questions.stem: How does X-Ray apply the same logic as the file-level hotspot map, but one level down?

options.text: It uses the same principal (complexity) times interest (change frequency) logic to rank functions instead of files
It switches to ranking functions purely by alphabetical name
It abandons change frequency and ranks only by lines of code
It ranks functions by the number of comments they contain

questions.explanation: X-Ray applies the same principal-times-interest logic (complexity x change frequency) at the function level, making the refactoring target precise. B, C and D drop or replace the defining axes. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.18)

questions.stem: What does X-Ray let you avoid when the hotspot is a very large file?

options.text: Reading the file's documentation
Rewriting the entire 2,000-line file, by pinpointing the two functions doing the damage
Running any tests at all on the file
Using version control on the file

questions.explanation: X-Ray pinpoints the worst functions so you don't rewrite a whole large file; the fix is scoped to the offenders. The other options are not what X-Ray addresses. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.17-18)

questions.stem: What does CodeScene's 'change coupling' analysis reveal?

options.text: Files or functions that tend to change together in the same commits
Files that share the most lines of duplicated code
Files with the highest cyclomatic complexity
Files that were created on the same date

questions.explanation: Change coupling reveals files/functions that historically change together in the same commits — a temporal dependency. B is duplication, C is complexity, D is creation date; none is change coupling. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.19)

questions.stem: Why can change coupling catch dependencies that a static call-graph misses?

options.text: Because it counts the number of import statements
Because it inspects the compiler's symbol table
Because it is temporal — mined from the VCS — so it finds files that always change together even with no code reference between them
Because it analyses runtime stack traces

questions.explanation: Change coupling is temporal, mined from commit history, so it catches hidden dependencies (two files with no static reference that always co-change). D, A and B are static or runtime techniques that would miss such hidden coupling. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.19)

questions.stem: High change coupling is described as a form of debt because it corresponds to which Fowler code smell?

options.text: Long Method
Feature Envy
Primitive Obsession
Shotgun Surgery — one logical change smeared across many files

questions.explanation: High change coupling means a single logical change is spread across multiple files — Fowler's Shotgun Surgery smell, which the deck lists explicitly. The other smells describe different problems. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.19, p.24)

questions.stem: How does change coupling feed the change process's Impact Analysis phase?

options.text: It only matters after verification is complete
If A historically co-changes with B, B almost certainly belongs in A's impact set even when the static dependency graph misses it
It removes files from the impact set to make analysis faster
It replaces the need for concept location entirely

questions.explanation: Change coupling is a behavioural input to impact analysis: historical co-change means C likely belongs in A's impact set, correcting a static call-graph that under-counts it. C inverts its use; D overstates it; A misplaces it in the process. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.19)

questions.stem: Two files in JHotDraw have no static dependency between them but always appear together in the same commits. What does this indicate?

options.text: That the files have low cyclomatic complexity
That neither file is part of any hotspot
Hidden change coupling that a static dependency graph would miss but that belongs in the impact set
That the files are duplicates of each other

questions.explanation: Files with no static link that always co-change exhibit hidden change coupling, which a static graph misses but which belongs in the impact set. D, A and B draw unsupported conclusions about duplication, complexity, or hotspots. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.19)

questions.stem: The deck's section on legacy code argues which broad point about technical debt?

options.text: Technical debt is purely a matter of code complexity
Technical debt only exists in compiled languages
Technical debt can always be measured without the VCS
Technical debt isn't just technical — it has a social/organisational dimension

questions.explanation: The legacy-code section's headline is 'why technical debt isn't just technical', widening debt to its social/organisational dimension. A, B and C narrow or misstate the point. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.20)

questions.stem: How does the deck define legacy code?

options.text: Code that lacks quality (in a relative sense) and/or that we didn't write ourselves
Any code older than ten years
Code without a license file
Code that fails to compile

questions.explanation: Legacy code is defined by two dimensions: lacks quality (relative perspective) and/or we didn't write it ourselves (ownership). B equates legacy with age (the deck rejects this); C and D are unrelated. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.21)

questions.stem: Which scenario shows that code can become 'legacy' even when it is not intrinsically bad?

options.text: A heavily commented file with full test coverage and active owners
A small utility file that everyone on the team understands
A file that was just merged after thorough code review
A brand-new, well-written file whose only author has already left the team

questions.explanation: Because legacy is partly ownership-based, a well-written file becomes legacy if its author has left and the knowledge is gone. The other options describe well-owned, understood code, not legacy. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.21)

questions.stem: What does 'The Technical Debt That Wasn't' slide illustrate?

options.text: Abandoned/unowned code whose risk is organisational rather than in the code itself
A file with the highest cyclomatic complexity in the repo
Code that turned out to have no debt after static analysis
A file that was refactored to zero complexity

questions.explanation: The slide shows three products, one with no owner (a '?'): abandoned code whose risk is organisational, not in the code itself. B, C and D misread the slide's point about unowned code. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.21)

questions.stem: Why does the deck reframe legacy code as 'partly a people problem'?

options.text: Because the people dimension replaces all technical measurement
Because code becomes legacy not only when it is bad but when the knowledge of it leaves the organisation
Because only people, never code, can have defects
Because legacy code is always written by junior developers

questions.explanation: Legacy is partly a people problem because knowledge loss (authors leaving) turns code legacy regardless of intrinsic quality. C is false; D is an unfounded generalisation; A overstates the people dimension. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.21)

questions.stem: What question does CodeScene's off-boarding simulation answer?

options.text: Which programming language is fastest to compile?
How quickly can you turn your codebase into legacy code if specific developers leave?
How many lines of code can the team write per sprint?
How many static violations remain after a release?

questions.explanation: Off-boarding simulation answers how quickly the codebase becomes legacy if chosen developers depart, modelling knowledge loss. The other options are unrelated questions. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.22)

questions.stem: How does CodeScene perform an off-boarding simulation?

options.text: By running the test suite without their code
By counting the lines they personally wrote and removing them
By picking one or more developers and modelling their departure across the authorship map
By deleting their commits from the git history

questions.explanation: It picks developers (e.g. Brian Vaughn) and models their departure over the authorship map to predict what would become legacy. D, A and B describe destructive or unrelated operations, not the simulation. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.22)

questions.stem: Which of these is a category in the off-boarding map's legend?

options.text: Comment Density
Off-Boarding Risk
Cyclomatic Complexity
Duplication Percentage

questions.explanation: The legend distinguishes Knowledge, Current Loss, Simulated Loss, Off-Boarding Risk, and Inconclusive. The other options are static metrics, not legend categories in this view. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.22)

questions.stem: What organisational concept does the off-boarding simulation make quantitative and predictive?

options.text: The duplication ratio across the repository
The compile time of the project
Bus-factor exposure — seeing knowledge concentration before the knowledge walks out the door
The cyclomatic complexity ceiling of the codebase

questions.explanation: Off-boarding simulation makes bus-factor exposure quantitative and predictive, letting the organisation act (pair, document, redistribute) before knowledge leaves. The other options are unrelated technical metrics. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.23)

questions.stem: Before an author's simulated departure, the react directory glows blue; after, large regions turn red. What is the correct reading of the legend colours here?

options.text: Blue means high complexity; red means low complexity
Blue means the code is abandoned; red means the code is well owned
Blue and red both mean the code is fully tested
Blue means knowledge is present (still owned); red means off-boarding risk / would-be legacy

questions.explanation: Blue marks knowledge present (owned); red marks off-boarding risk / code that would become legacy. A confuses colour with complexity; B inverts the meanings; C is false. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.22-23)

questions.stem: What action does the off-boarding analysis enable an organisation to take before knowledge is lost?

options.text: Pair, document, or redistribute ownership of at-risk code
Delete the at-risk code immediately
Stop using version control for that code
Increase the file's cyclomatic complexity deliberately

questions.explanation: By seeing bus-factor exposure in advance, a team can pair, document, or redistribute ownership before the author leaves. B, C and D are counterproductive or irrelevant. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.22-23)

questions.stem: Which cause of technical debt does the Tower-of-Babel slide specifically dramatise?

options.text: Excessive automated testing
Using too many design patterns
Frequent pair programming
Age and historical accretion — debt builds up so long that the problem is knowing where to start

questions.explanation: The Tower-of-Babel slide dramatises age and historical accretion ('thousands of years of technical debt'), where the challenge is where to start. The other options are not causes the deck identifies. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.8)

questions.stem: Which cause of debt is invisible to static tools but surfaced by behavioural analysis, per the guide's causes list?

options.text: Magic numbers in conditionals
Hidden coupling — changes that spread across co-changing files (Shotgun Surgery)
High line count in a single file
Deep inheritance hierarchies

questions.explanation: Hidden coupling (co-changing files, Shotgun Surgery) is invisible to static tools but surfaced by change-coupling analysis. C, D and A are statically visible code-quality issues, not the hidden coupling cause. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.19)

questions.stem: Per the guide, which cause of debt is rooted in schedule pressure and shortcuts?

options.text: Ignoring complexity / taking shortcuts that compound and slow every sprint
Writing too many tests under deadline
Refactoring too aggressively
Adding too much documentation

questions.explanation: Ignoring complexity under schedule pressure (Hickey) is a listed cause: shortcuts compound and slow every sprint. B, C and D are not causes the deck attributes debt to. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.4)

questions.stem: What is the first prescriptive strategy the deck offers for repaying debt (the Tower-of-Babel lesson)?

options.text: Repay debt only in the largest files first
Don't try to fix everything — choose a starting point
Rewrite the entire codebase from scratch
Fix files in alphabetical order

questions.explanation: The Tower-of-Babel slide frames blanket repayment as hopeless: you must choose a starting point, not try to fix everything. C, D and A are exactly the boil-the-ocean or arbitrary approaches the deck warns against. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.8)

questions.stem: How does the deck recommend validating a hotspot-based prioritisation?

options.text: By counting total lines of code in the project
By measuring the build time
By surveying users about their favourite features
With fault history — direct effort where change history predicts the most faults (Graves et al.)

questions.explanation: Validate the prioritisation with fault history: change history predicts faults, so hotspots are also the most defect-prone (Graves et al.). The other options do not validate fault risk. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.14)

questions.stem: Why does the deck advise watching trends rather than single snapshots when managing debt?

options.text: Because a single snapshot is always more accurate than a trend
Because trends only matter after a file is deleted
Because rising complexity against flat LOC signals compounding debt you should act on before the file becomes unmanageable
Because trends are easier to ignore than snapshots

questions.explanation: Trends reveal compounding debt (rising complexity vs flat LOC) so you act before the file becomes unmanageable. D, A and B dismiss or invert the value of trend analysis. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.15)

questions.stem: How does the deck say you should manage the social half of debt?

options.text: Increase the cyclomatic complexity to discourage changes
Remove version control to prevent knowledge tracking
Assign every file to exactly one permanent owner
Reduce knowledge loss and off-boarding risk by pairing, documenting, and redistributing ownership, using off-boarding simulation

questions.explanation: Manage social debt by reducing knowledge loss and off-boarding risk (pair, document, redistribute) using off-boarding simulation. A and B are counterproductive; C increases bus-factor risk rather than reducing it. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.22-23)

questions.stem: What is the deck's concluding rule about the relationship between data/tools and human judgement?

options.text: Let data support, not replace, judgement — rely on human expertise to get the highest ROI
Let the tool make every refactoring decision automatically
Ignore data entirely and rely only on intuition
Refactor whatever the tool marks reddest, without discussion

questions.explanation: The conclusion is that data should support, not replace, judgement: rely on human expertise for the highest ROI. B and D let the tool decide (the wrong lesson); C discards the data the deck values. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.25)

questions.stem: The deck's title 'Beyond Technical Debt' signals three ways the lecture goes 'beyond'. Which is one of them?

options.text: Beyond the language — using only compiled languages
Beyond the team — removing all human input
Beyond the repository — analysing code without version control
Beyond the snapshot — from a static debt figure to an evolutionary movie adding time and people

questions.explanation: One of the three 'beyonds' is beyond the snapshot (static figure to evolutionary movie). The others are beyond the code (social debt) and beyond the tool (human judgement); the distractors are not the deck's framings. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.9-11)

questions.stem: Which is another of the deck's three 'beyond' framings?

options.text: Beyond the code — debt is also social: legacy code, knowledge loss, off-boarding risk
Beyond the budget — debt has no monetary cost
Beyond the user — debt never reaches the product
Beyond the metric — debt cannot be measured

questions.explanation: Another framing is beyond the code: debt is also social (legacy, knowledge loss, off-boarding). B, C and D contradict the deck (debt has cost, reaches users, and is measurable). (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.20)

questions.stem: Which is the third of the deck's 'beyond' framings?

options.text: Beyond the tests — testing is unnecessary
Beyond the tool — data doesn't decide for you; rely on human expertise for highest ROI
Beyond the team — automate all decisions
Beyond the code review — skip review entirely

questions.explanation: The third framing is beyond the tool: data supports but does not replace human expertise. C, D and A are not the deck's positions. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.25)

questions.stem: What does the title-slide image (a tortoise on a long road) symbolise for the lecture?

options.text: Debt slows you down for the long haul
Debt is removed instantly with the right tool
Debt only affects fast-moving teams
Debt has no effect on delivery speed

questions.explanation: The tortoise on a long road symbolises that debt slows you down for the long haul — the lecture's theme. B, C and D contradict the metaphor. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.1)

questions.stem: The 'There's Much More in CodeScene' slide lists further capabilities. Which is one of them?

options.text: Encrypting the source repository
Microservices analysis (shotgun surgery, team conflicts, technical sprawl)
Automatic code generation from comments
Compiling the project to native binaries

questions.explanation: The much-more slide lists change coupling, microservices analysis (with shotgun surgery, team conflicts, technical sprawl), proactive warnings, retrospectives, delivery performance, and branch analyses. C, D and A are not capabilities the deck names. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.24)

questions.stem: Under the 'microservices' sub-points on the much-more slide, which three issues are listed?

options.text: SQL injection, XSS, and CSRF
Cache misses, GC pauses, and thread starvation
Shotgun surgery, team conflicts, and technical sprawl
Memory leaks, race conditions, and deadlocks

questions.explanation: The microservices sub-points are shotgun surgery, team conflicts, and technical sprawl. D, A and B list runtime/security concerns not on this slide. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.24)

questions.stem: Which is one of the four concluding messages of the deck?

options.text: Technical debt is purely an aesthetic preference
Technical debt is a real, language-independent problem
Technical debt only affects Java codebases
Technical debt disappears with enough comments

questions.explanation: A concluding message is that debt is a real, language-independent problem. C, D and A contradict the deck's conclusions. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.25)

questions.stem: According to the conclusion, what does the version-control system hold?

options.text: Only the latest snapshot of the code
Only compiled binaries
A huge amount of useful information about the codebase
Nothing relevant to technical-debt analysis

questions.explanation: The conclusion states the VCS holds a huge amount of useful information (history, authorship, change frequency). D, A and B deny the rich evolutionary data the deck mines. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.25)

questions.stem: What is the conclusion's stance on the role of human expertise?

options.text: Human expertise should be replaced by the tool's recommendations
Human expertise is irrelevant once metrics exist
Human expertise only matters for writing new code, not managing debt
You must ultimately rely on human expertise; data supports developers' judgement for highest ROI

questions.explanation: The conclusion is emphatic that you must rely on human expertise, with data supporting judgement for the highest ROI. A, B and C all sideline the human role the deck insists on. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.25)

questions.stem: The conclusion says the right role of data is to do what?

options.text: Support developers' judgement and experience to get the highest ROI
Replace developers' judgement to remove human error
Generate a single debt percentage for management
Automate refactoring without developer involvement

questions.explanation: Data's right role is to support developers' judgement and experience for the highest ROI. B and D overstate automation; C reduces data to the snapshot figure the deck criticises. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.25)

questions.stem: How does the guide position behavioural debt analysis relative to Prefactoring and Postfactoring?

options.text: As a replacement for refactoring that removes the need to change code
As a phase that runs only after the system is retired
As a tool that automatically performs the refactoring itself
As the prioritisation layer that decides which debt to repay so refactoring effort lands where the next change is most expensive

questions.explanation: Behavioural analysis is the prioritisation layer over Prefactoring/Postfactoring: it decides which debt to repay so effort lands where the next Actualization is most expensive. A, B and C misstate its role; it directs, not replaces, refactoring. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.12)

questions.stem: What is the division of labour between L04 (Refactoring) and L11 (Technical Debt), per the guide?

options.text: L04 = how to refactor (the repayment mechanism); L11 = which debt to repay first and when (the economics/prioritisation)
L04 = which debt to repay; L11 = how to refactor
Both lectures cover only how to write unit tests
L04 = static analysis; L11 = compilation

questions.explanation: L04 supplies the repayment mechanism (how to refactor); L11 supplies the economics (which debt first and when). B swaps the two; C and D are unrelated. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.12)

questions.stem: In the worked example, which steps does the guide map onto Concept Location and Impact Analysis?

options.text: The behavioural-enrichment steps (mapping hotspots and checking change coupling) tell you which code is expensive and which files co-change
Only the final refactoring step maps to concept location
None of the steps relate to the change process
The off-boarding simulation alone maps to concept location

questions.explanation: The guide maps the behavioural-enrichment steps (hotspot mapping, change coupling) onto Concept Location and Impact Analysis — what code is expensive and what co-changes. B, C and D mis-map the worked example. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.13-19)

questions.stem: Why does an impacted hotspot raise the risk and cost of a change request?

options.text: Because hotspots have no relationship to fault density
Because the change lands in expensive, frequently-churned, complex code, increasing prefactoring need and regression risk
Because hotspots are always read-only and cannot be changed
Because hotspots reduce the size of the impact set

questions.explanation: An impacted hotspot means the change lands in complex, high-churn code, so prefactoring effort and regression risk both rise. C, D and A contradict what a hotspot implies for a change. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.12, p.14)

questions.stem: In technical-debt terms, why is JHotDraw described as a 'low-debt baseline'?

options.text: It is the largest and most-changed codebase in the course
It deliberately maximises cyclomatic complexity
Its principal (complexity) was kept low by deliberate pattern-rich, maintainable design
It has never been placed under version control

questions.explanation: JHotDraw is the course's exemplar of clean, pattern-rich, maintainable code, so its principal was kept low by design — the opposite of a red-hot hotspot. D, A and B mischaracterise JHotDraw. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.13)

questions.stem: If you ran CodeScene over JHotDraw's git history, where would the guide expect any hotspots to cluster?

options.text: In its README and documentation files
In files that have never been modified
Uniformly across every file regardless of change history
In its most-changed, most-complex areas (e.g. the Figure/drawing-editor hierarchy or command/undo machinery)

questions.explanation: The same principal x interest rule applies: hotspots would cluster in JHotDraw's most-changed, most-complex areas (Figure hierarchy, command/undo). A, B and C ignore the complexity-times-frequency definition. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.13)

questions.stem: Why does the guide call JHotDraw 'the right contrast' for the technical-debt lecture?

options.text: JHotDraw has no design patterns, unlike the lecture's examples
JHotDraw is written in the same way as the red-hot checker.ts hotspot
JHotDraw illustrates the preventive half (avoid borrowing) while this lecture covers the diagnostic/repayment half (find and pay back existing debt)
JHotDraw demonstrates how to accumulate debt quickly

questions.explanation: JHotDraw shows the preventive half of the story (good design avoids borrowing) while L11 covers the diagnostic/repayment half, so together they bracket maintainability. D, A and B invert JHotDraw's role as a clean exemplar. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.13)

questions.stem: Which statement is the 'conflating principal with cost' pitfall the guide warns against?

options.text: Treating a frequently-changed complex file as a hotspot
Measuring cost as principal times interest
Prioritising the most-changed-recently file
Treating a complex file you never touch as costly, when dormant debt charges no interest

questions.explanation: The pitfall is conflating principal with cost: a complex file you never touch is dormant and charges no interest, so cost is principal x interest, not complexity alone. A, B and C are the correct behaviours, not the pitfall. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.12)

questions.stem: Which is the 'trusting the snapshot as actionable' pitfall?

options.text: Mistaking the Sonar dashboard's precision (162,306 LOC, 683 man-days) for usefulness in deciding where to start
Using change frequency to rank files
Drilling into functions with X-Ray
Watching complexity trends over time

questions.explanation: The pitfall is mistaking precision for usefulness: the Sonar dashboard is precise but, as the slide title 'Actionable?' asks, gives no starting point. B, C and D are recommended practices, not the pitfall. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.7-8)

questions.stem: Which is the 'forgetting that change frequency beats size for faults' pitfall?

options.text: Prioritising the most-changed-recently file
Weighting recent changes most heavily
Using process measures from the change history
Prioritising the biggest file instead of the most-changed-recently one

questions.explanation: The pitfall is prioritising the biggest file when the research shows change frequency (and recency) beats size for faults. A, B and C are the correct, research-aligned behaviours. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.14)

questions.stem: Which is the 'acting on a single data point instead of the trend' pitfall?

options.text: Judging a file by its current complexity snapshot rather than its trajectory
Judging a file by its rising complexity-vs-flat-LOC trend
Treating the tool as a movie
Acting before a file becomes unmanageable

questions.explanation: The pitfall is acting on a single snapshot rather than the trend; the trajectory (rising complexity vs flat LOC) is the real alarm. B, C and D are the recommended trend-aware behaviours. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.15)

questions.stem: Which is the 'treating debt as purely technical' pitfall?

options.text: Ignoring knowledge loss and off-boarding risk because debt is assumed to be only about code
Accounting for knowledge loss and off-boarding risk
Simulating off-boarding before an author leaves
Recognising that legacy is partly an ownership problem

questions.explanation: The pitfall is treating debt as purely technical and ignoring the people dimension (knowledge loss, off-boarding). B, C and D correctly account for the social half. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.20-23)

questions.stem: Which is the 'letting the tool make the decision' pitfall?

options.text: Choosing the highest-ROI fix after analysis
Blindly refactoring whatever is reddest without human judgement
Using data to support a developer's judgement
Relying ultimately on human expertise

questions.explanation: The pitfall is letting the tool decide — blindly refactoring whatever is reddest. The conclusion insists data supports human judgement, so C, D and A are the correct stance. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.25)

questions.stem: Which is the 'missing hidden coupling in impact analysis' pitfall?

options.text: Adding co-changing files to the impact set
Using VCS history to correct the impact set
Treating change coupling as a temporal dependency
Relying on a static call-graph that under-counts the impact set by missing files that always change together

questions.explanation: The pitfall is missing hidden coupling: a static call-graph under-counts the impact set because it misses files that always co-change; the VCS history corrects this. A, B and C are the correct corrective behaviours. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.19)

questions.stem: Per the definitions table, 'Principal' is measured as which of the following?

options.text: Percentage of comments
Code complexity (size, cyclomatic complexity)
Change frequency in the VCS
Number of owners of the file

questions.explanation: Principal = the substandard code itself, measured as code complexity (size, cyclomatic complexity). Change frequency is the interest rate; ownership and comments are different dimensions. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.12)

questions.stem: Per the definitions table, 'Interest rate' is measured as which of the following?

options.text: Lines of code
Number of static violations
Change frequency — how often the code is modified in the VCS
Cyclomatic complexity of the code

questions.explanation: Interest rate = the recurring cost of the principal, measured as change frequency (how often code is modified in the VCS). The other options are complexity or snapshot metrics. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.12)

questions.stem: Per the definitions table, what is a 'snapshot' in conventional analysis?

options.text: An evolutionary view over the VCS history adding time and people
A ranked list of functions by complexity x change frequency
A chord diagram of co-changing files
A static, point-in-time report of violations/debt that tells you how much, not where to start

questions.explanation: A snapshot is a static, point-in-time report (e.g. SonarQube's 11% / 683 man days) — how much, not where to start. A is the movie; B is X-Ray; C is change coupling. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.6-8)

questions.stem: Per the definitions table, what is the 'movie' in behavioural analysis?

options.text: An evolutionary view over the VCS history adding the time aspect and organization & people dimensions
A static point-in-time violation report
A single debt percentage figure
A compiled binary of the codebase

questions.explanation: The movie is an evolutionary view over VCS history adding time and organization & people (CodeScene). B and C are the snapshot; D is unrelated. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.9-10)

questions.stem: Per the definitions table, who created CodeScene?

options.text: Rich Hickey
Martin Fowler
Adam Tornhill
Ward Cunningham

questions.explanation: CodeScene is Adam Tornhill's behavioural code-analysis tool. Cunningham coined the debt metaphor; Hickey gave the complexity talk; Fowler wrote Refactoring. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.9-11)

questions.stem: Per the definitions table, what is 'complexity trend'?

options.text: The single current cyclomatic-complexity value of a file
The total number of commits to a file
The number of authors over the file's lifetime
The trajectory of complexity over time; rising complexity against flat LOC signals compounding debt

questions.explanation: Complexity trend is the trajectory of complexity over time; rising complexity vs flat LOC signals compounding debt. A is a snapshot value; B and C are different evolutionary metrics. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.15)

questions.stem: Per the definitions table, what is 'knowledge loss / abandoned code'?

options.text: Code whose authors are gone, measurable from the VCS; becomes legacy regardless of intrinsic quality
Code that fails static analysis
Code with high cyclomatic complexity
Code that lacks unit tests

questions.explanation: Knowledge loss / abandoned code is code whose authors are gone (measurable from the VCS), becoming legacy regardless of its quality. B, C and D describe other quality issues, not abandonment. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.15, p.21)

questions.stem: Per the definitions table, what is 'off-boarding risk / simulation'?

options.text: Measuring the test coverage of new hires' code
Predicting how much code would become legacy if specific developers left, by simulating their departure over the authorship map
Estimating how long a build takes after a developer leaves
Counting the number of merge conflicts per release

questions.explanation: Off-boarding risk/simulation predicts how much code becomes legacy if developers leave, by simulating departure over the authorship map. C, D and A are unrelated measurements. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.22-23)

questions.stem: Per the definitions table, what is 'The technical debt that wasn't'?

options.text: Abandoned/unowned code whose risk is organisational rather than in the code itself
Debt that was fully repaid before it accrued interest
A file with zero cyclomatic complexity
A bug that turned out not to exist

questions.explanation: 'The technical debt that wasn't' is abandoned/unowned code whose risk is organisational, not in the code itself. B, C and D misread the phrase. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.21)

questions.stem: Per the definitions table, what is 'Shotgun surgery'?

options.text: A version-control merge strategy
A code smell where one logical change is spread across many files; surfaces as high change coupling
A refactoring that splits one class into many
A testing technique for rapid regression checks

questions.explanation: Shotgun surgery is the smell where one logical change spreads across many files, surfacing as high change coupling. C, D and A are unrelated to the smell. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.24)

questions.stem: Per the definitions table, what does 'Code, Process and Evolutionary metrics' refer to?

options.text: The three sides of the impact slide
The three colours used in the hotspot map
Inputs derived from source + version control + JIRA, fed through pattern detectors/ML to produce priorities and predictions
The three Lehman laws used in the deck

questions.explanation: Code/Process/Evolutionary metrics are inputs from source + VCS + JIRA, fed through pattern detectors/ML to produce priorities and predictions. The distractors mislabel unrelated triples. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.11)

questions.stem: Per the definitions table, what does X-Ray do?

options.text: Reports a single global debt percentage
Renders the whole codebase as nested circles
Simulates a developer leaving the team
Drills inside a hotspot file to rank its functions by change frequency x cyclomatic complexity and show change coupling

questions.explanation: X-Ray is a deep dive that ranks a hotspot file's functions by change frequency x cyclomatic complexity and shows change coupling. A is the snapshot figure; B is the hotspot map; C is off-boarding simulation. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.16-18)

questions.stem: Your SonarQube report says 11% technical debt / 683 man-days. Why is this insufficient for planning repayment, and what should you do instead?

options.text: It is too low; instead double the estimate and rewrite everything
It is a snapshot that omits where to start; instead map hotspots (complexity x change frequency) and repay the highest-interest first
It overstates the debt; instead halve every figure and proceed
It is fine as-is; just allocate 683 man-days evenly across all files

questions.explanation: The snapshot says how much but not where to start; the fix is hotspot prioritisation (complexity x change frequency), repaying the highest-interest debt first. C, D and A either mis-handle the figure or boil the ocean. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.7-8, p.12)

questions.stem: Which sequence correctly orders the deck's worked-example process for finding and prioritising the worst debt?

options.text: Fix every violation alphabetically, then map hotspots
Simulate off-boarding first, then ignore complexity entirely
Reject the snapshot, map hotspots, validate with fault history, read the trend, X-Ray to functions, check change coupling, account for social debt, repay via refactoring
Rewrite the codebase, then run static analysis, then stop

questions.explanation: The worked example proceeds: reject snapshot -> map hotspots -> validate with fault history -> read trend -> X-Ray to functions -> check change coupling -> account for social debt -> repay via refactoring. D, A and B scramble or omit the steps. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.8-25)

questions.stem: How would you concisely explain why technical debt is also a business problem?

options.text: It only slows down individual developers, never the business
It increases the codebase's line count, which pleases the business
It is a purely cosmetic concern with no business impact
It causes long lead times and unpredictability (business) plus bugs (product), and complexity kills velocity (Hickey)

questions.explanation: Debt is a business problem because it causes long lead times and unpredictability (business), bugs (product), and complexity kills velocity (Hickey). A, B and C deny or trivialise the business impact. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.4-5)

questions.stem: A team picks the reddest hotspot and immediately starts a full rewrite without checking ownership or trends. Which two lessons of the deck are they violating?

options.text: Prioritise by size, and trust the snapshot figure
Refactor complex code you never touch, and skip fault history
X-Ray to scope the fix (don't rewrite the whole file) and account for social debt / ownership before acting
Always rewrite the whole file, and ignore the people dimension

questions.explanation: They violate two lessons: scope via X-Ray rather than rewriting wholesale, and account for ownership/social debt before acting. D restates the mistakes; A and B name unrelated wrong behaviours. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.17-23)

questions.stem: Which definition should be memorised 'verbatim-ish' for a hotspot, per the exam focus?

options.text: The largest file in the repository by line count
Any file flagged by more than ten static rules
Code that has not been changed in over a year
Complicated code that you have to work with often

questions.explanation: The exam-focus verbatim hotspot definition is 'complicated code that you have to work with often.' A, B and C are not the deck's definition. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.12)

questions.stem: Which pair of properties together define a hotspot, and which is the principal vs the interest?

options.text: Code complexity (principal) and change frequency (interest)
Change frequency (principal) and code complexity (interest)
Ownership (principal) and duplication (interest)
Lines of code (principal) and comment density (interest)

questions.explanation: A hotspot = code complexity (principal) x change frequency (interest). B swaps the roles; C and D substitute non-defining metrics. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.12)

questions.stem: Why is putting change frequency at the centre of debt prioritisation defensible rather than arbitrary?

options.text: Because change frequency is easier to compute than complexity
Because empirical fault-prediction research (Graves et al.) shows change frequency and recency predict faults better than size
Because change frequency is the only metric a VCS can produce
Because complexity cannot be measured at all

questions.explanation: Centring change frequency is defensible because Graves et al. show it (and recency) predicts faults better than size — the evidential basis for the interest-rate axis. C, D and A give weak or false justifications. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.14)

questions.stem: Which best distinguishes maintainability, technical debt, and a hotspot as the guide relates them?

options.text: A hotspot is the quality attribute; maintainability is the deficit; debt is the tool
Maintainability is the quality attribute; technical debt is the deficit in it; a hotspot is debt that is actively costing you (high interest)
All three are synonyms for cyclomatic complexity
Maintainability is the deficit; technical debt is the attribute; a hotspot is dormant debt

questions.explanation: Maintainability is the attribute, technical debt the deficit in it, and a hotspot is debt actively costing interest. C collapses the distinctions; D and A scramble the mappings. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.12)

questions.stem: What is the relationship between code smells (e.g. Shotgun Surgery) and behavioural metrics (hotspots, change coupling)?

options.text: Behavioural metrics are qualitative; smells are quantitative
Neither relates to technical debt
Code smells are qualitative debt indicators; behavioural metrics are the quantitative, prioritised version
They are identical and interchangeable

questions.explanation: Code smells are qualitative debt indicators while behavioural metrics (hotspots, change coupling) are the quantitative, prioritised version. D denies the distinction; A inverts it; B is false. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.19, p.24)

questions.stem: Roughly how many lines of code and commits did the checker.ts hotspot have on the trend slide?

options.text: About 100 lines and 5 commits
About 25,774 lines and 785 commits
About 1,000 lines and 50 commits
About 162,306 lines and 10,072 commits

questions.explanation: checker.ts had about 25,774 LOC and 785 commits. The 162,306/10,072 figures belong to the Sonar dashboard, not checker.ts; the other counts are too small. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.15)

questions.stem: In the X-Ray attach.* table, the top-level 'attach' context had the highest change frequency. What was it?

options.text: 48 changes
785 changes
103 changes
9 changes

questions.explanation: The top-level attach context had 103 changes (highest change frequency), with cyclomatic complexity 9. 48 is updateFiberRecursively's complexity; 785 is checker.ts's commits; 9 is attach's complexity, not its change count. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.18)

questions.stem: Which open-source codebase is used for the nested-circle hotspot map on slide 13?

options.text: TypeScript compiler
JHotDraw
Linux kernel
ReactJS

questions.explanation: Slide 13's hotspot map is ReactJS (packages like react-dom, react-reconciler). TypeScript's checker.ts is the trend slide; JHotDraw is not used by the deck; the Linux kernel does not appear. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.13)

questions.stem: Which codebase supplies the 'worrisome trend' example (checker.ts)?

options.text: TypeScript compiler
ReactJS
JHotDraw
Drawlets

questions.explanation: The worrisome-trend example is TypeScript's src/compiler/checker.ts. ReactJS is the hotspot map and X-Ray example; JHotDraw and Drawlets are course examples not used in this deck. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.15)

questions.stem: Which codebase supplies the X-Ray attach.* function-table example?

options.text: JHotDraw Figure hierarchy
The Sonar demo project
A React (react-devtools) attach.* module
TypeScript checker.ts

questions.explanation: The X-Ray function table is a React attach.* module. checker.ts is the trend slide; JHotDraw is not in the deck; the Sonar project is the snapshot example. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.18)

questions.stem: On the Sonar 'Actionable?' dashboard, which figure was reported for rules compliance?
questions.explanation: Rules compliance was 83.7%. 11.0% is the technical-debt percentage, 26.6% is comments, and 7.1% is duplications on the same dashboard. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.7)

questions.stem: What was checker.ts's defect figure on the trend slide?

options.text: 0 defects
1,051 defects (133% bug fixes)
10,072 defects
683 defects

questions.explanation: checker.ts showed 1,051 defects (133% bug fixes). 10,072 is the Sonar violation count, 683 is man-days, and 0 misstates the figure. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.15)

questions.stem: A static analyser flags File A (complexity 90, last changed 2 years ago) and File B (complexity 40, changed 30 times this month). Which is the better hotspot candidate?

options.text: Both equally, since only the static analyser's flag matters
File B, because its frequent recent change gives it high interest despite lower complexity
File A, because its higher complexity automatically makes it the hotspot
Neither, because hotspots ignore complexity entirely

questions.explanation: File B's frequent recent change (high interest) makes it the better hotspot candidate even with lower complexity; File A's dormant principal charges little interest. C reads complexity alone; D drops complexity; A ignores the two axes. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.12-14)

questions.stem: Why does the guide say a static call-graph impact analysis will 'under-count' the impact set?

options.text: Because impact sets should only ever contain one file
