Because change coupling removes files from the impact set
Because files with no static dependency can still always change together (change coupling), which the call-graph misses
Because static call-graphs over-report dependencies

questions.explanation: A static call-graph misses hidden change coupling — files with no static link that always co-change — so it under-counts the impact set; VCS history corrects it. D inverts the error; A and B misstate impact-set behaviour. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.19)

questions.stem: Which statement about complexity and 'whether it matters' reflects the deck's view?

options.text: Complexity alone is always sufficient to flag a problem
Change frequency alone, ignoring complexity, fully defines a hotspot
Neither complexity nor change frequency is relevant to debt
Complexity is necessary but not sufficient to flag a problem; you also need the change-frequency dimension

questions.explanation: Complexity is necessary but not sufficient; you need change frequency to know if it matters (static analysis can't tell you alone). A over-credits complexity; B drops complexity; C denies both axes. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.10, p.12)

questions.stem: Why is fixing change coupling described as addressing Shotgun Surgery, and why does that reduce debt?

options.text: Because change coupling is a runtime performance bug
Because co-changing files are always exact duplicates to be deleted
Because co-changing files mean one logical change is smeared across many files; consolidating it reduces the per-change effort and risk
Because Shotgun Surgery means a single file is too large and must be split

questions.explanation: High change coupling is Shotgun Surgery (one change spread across many files); reducing it cuts the effort and risk of each future change, lowering debt. D is Long Method, not Shotgun Surgery; A and B misclassify the smell. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.19, p.24)

questions.stem: What is the correct interpretation of 'Code Health 1' for a file?

options.text: The file is in the healthiest band and needs no attention
The file has exactly one author
The file has been changed only once
The file is in the lowest (unhealthiest) band and warrants attention

questions.explanation: Code Health 1 is the lowest, unhealthiest band (shown red), warranting attention. A inverts the scale; B and C confuse it with authorship or change count. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.15)

questions.stem: How does the hotspot map help a developer compared with a flat list of 10,072 violations?

options.text: It directs attention to a handful of high-interest files instead of an undifferentiated list
It guarantees the violations will fix themselves
It removes the need to ever look at the code
It lists violations in strict severity order only

questions.explanation: The map directs attention to the handful of high-interest hotspots rather than an undifferentiated 10,072-item list. B and C are absurd; D describes the snapshot tool, not the map. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.7, p.13)

questions.stem: Two functions in a hotspot file: F1 (complexity 48, changed 24 times) and F2 (complexity 9, changed 103 times). Using X-Ray's logic, how should you reason about them?

options.text: Only F2 matters, because change count is the only axis X-Ray uses
Neither matters, because X-Ray ignores both complexity and change frequency
Both are candidates: F1 is a high-principal target and F2 is a high-interest target; X-Ray surfaces both for judgement
Only F1 matters, because complexity is the only axis X-Ray uses

questions.explanation: X-Ray ranks by both change frequency and complexity, so F1 (high principal) and F2 (high interest) are both surfaced for the developer's judgement. D and A drop one axis; B denies X-Ray's defining metrics. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.18)

questions.stem: Which best states why the deck calls technical debt 'language-independent'?

options.text: Because all programming languages have identical syntax
Because debt only appears in dynamically typed languages
Because the deck only analysed one language
The hotspot, change-coupling and ownership patterns are mined from VCS history, which exists regardless of programming language

questions.explanation: Debt is language-independent because the behavioural patterns are mined from VCS history (commits, authorship), which exists for any language. A, B and C are false. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.25)

questions.stem: Per the worked example, after X-Raying to the two worst functions, when is the resulting work called Prefactoring versus Postfactoring?

options.text: Neither term applies to hotspot repayment
Prefactoring if it precedes a feature change; Postfactoring if it follows one
Prefactoring if it follows a change; Postfactoring if it precedes one
Both terms mean the same thing in this context

questions.explanation: The repayment is Prefactoring when it precedes a feature change and Postfactoring when it follows one. C inverts the definitions; D and A deny the distinction the change process makes. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.12)

questions.stem: Which of the following correctly pairs a CodeScene view with what it answers?

options.text: Off-boarding simulation -> how quickly the codebase becomes legacy if developers leave
Hotspot map -> which developer wrote each line
Change coupling -> the absolute debt percentage
Complexity trend -> the number of current owners

questions.explanation: Off-boarding simulation answers how quickly the codebase becomes legacy if developers leave. The other pairings mismatch each view with the wrong question. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.22)

questions.stem: How does the guide connect a hotspot inside an impact set to the verification effort for a change?

options.text: Because verification is unrelated to where faults concentrate
Because hotspots are always excluded from the impact set
Because faults concentrate in hotspots, verification must be most rigorous where the change touches a hotspot
Because hotspots cannot contain faults, verification can be relaxed there

questions.explanation: Since change history shows faults concentrate in hotspots, verification must be most rigorous where a change touches one. D, A and B contradict the fault-concentration link. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.14)

questions.stem: What does the guide mean by calling behavioural analysis a 'behavioural enrichment' of concept location and impact analysis?

options.text: It replaces concept location and impact analysis entirely
It removes the need for any source-code reading
It only applies after the change has shipped
It adds VCS-mined evidence (which code is expensive, which files co-change) on top of the static search those phases use

questions.explanation: Behavioural enrichment adds VCS-mined evidence (cost, co-change) on top of the static search in concept location and impact analysis. A and B overstate it; C mis-times it. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.13-19)

questions.stem: Adam Tornhill's body of work that underpins CodeScene is associated with which book title?

options.text: Refactoring
Your Code as a Crime Scene
Building Evolutionary Architectures
Simple Made Easy

questions.explanation: CodeScene grows out of Adam Tornhill's Your Code as a Crime Scene work. Building Evolutionary Architectures supplied the debt definition; Simple Made Easy is Hickey's talk; Refactoring is Fowler's book. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.26)

questions.stem: Why does the guide say the snapshot's '683 man-days' figure is not actionable on its own?

options.text: It already ranks files by change frequency
It cannot be computed for large codebases
It quantifies total effort but does not say which code to fix first for the most return
It is always an underestimate by a factor of ten

questions.explanation: The 683 man-days quantifies total effort but gives no prioritisation — which code to fix first for the most return. D, A and B mischaracterise the figure. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.7)

questions.stem: In the principal/interest table, how is the 'interest rate' shown on the hotspot diagram's axis?

options.text: As reddening circles along a 'Code Change Frequency' axis
As growing circles along a 'Code Complexity' axis
As the number of owners listed beside each file
As the comment percentage of each file

questions.explanation: The interest rate (change frequency) is shown as reddening circles along the Code Change Frequency axis; growing circles along Code Complexity is the principal. Owners and comments are not the diagram's axes. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.12)

questions.stem: A team measures only total complexity (principal) and refactors the most complex files, ignoring change frequency. What flaw does the deck predict?

options.text: They will perfectly minimise future maintenance cost
They will eliminate all faults because complexity is the only fault predictor
They may spend effort on dormant debt while leaving high-interest, frequently-changed files unaddressed
They will always fix the most expensive debt first

questions.explanation: Refactoring by complexity alone risks fixing dormant debt while ignoring high-interest churned files; the deck stresses cost is principal x interest. D and A are false; B contradicts Graves et al. (change frequency beats size). (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.12-14)

questions.stem: What does the deck mean by saying CodeScene produces 'predictive analytics' as an output?

options.text: It can predict things like which code would become legacy if an author left, not just describe the current state
It predicts the stock price of the company
It predicts the compile time of the next build
It predicts the weather for the release date

questions.explanation: Predictive analytics includes things like off-boarding prediction (which code becomes legacy if an author leaves), going beyond description. B, C and D are absurd distractors. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.11, p.22-23)

questions.stem: Why does the deck argue precision (e.g. 10,072 violations, EUR 341,563) should not be mistaken for usefulness?

options.text: Precise totals are the only thing needed to plan repayment
Precise totals still don't tell you where to start, which is the actual decision a team must make
Precise totals are always wrong by construction
Precise totals already encode change frequency and ownership

questions.explanation: Precise totals (violations, euro cost) don't answer the real question — where to start — so precision is not usefulness. C overstates; D credits the snapshot with behavioural data it lacks; A contradicts the whole critique. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.7-8)

questions.stem: Which best describes the 'time aspect' dimension CodeScene adds?

options.text: The time of day commits are made
The evolution of the code over the version-control history
The wall-clock time the analysis takes to run
The release date scheduled on the roadmap

questions.explanation: The time aspect is the evolution of the code over the VCS history. C, D and A misread 'time' as run time, schedule, or clock time. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.10)

questions.stem: A reviewer claims 'all of the codebase's 11% debt should be repaid evenly.' Using the deck, which rebuttal is strongest?

options.text: All debt is equal, so even repayment is optimal
Repay only the files that are never changed first
Even repayment ignores interest; effort should concentrate on hotspots where complexity meets high change frequency
The 11% figure is too low, so repay 22% evenly

questions.explanation: Even repayment ignores the interest dimension; effort should concentrate on hotspots (complexity x high change frequency). D mishandles the figure; A and B contradict the hotspot principle. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.8, p.12)

questions.stem: Why does the guide describe legacy code's 'ownership' criterion as a knowledge dimension?

options.text: Because ownership is a legal copyright matter only
Because ownership determines the file's cyclomatic complexity
Because ownership is measured by lines of comments
Because code we didn't write ourselves carries less knowledge, and that knowledge can be lost when authors leave

questions.explanation: Ownership is a knowledge dimension: code we didn't write carries less knowledge, which can be lost when authors leave. A reduces it to copyright; B and C tie it to unrelated metrics. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.21)

questions.stem: Why is off-boarding simulation called 'predictive' rather than merely descriptive?

options.text: Because it predicts how many tests will pass
Because it forecasts the project's release date
Because it models a future departure to forecast which code would become legacy, before the knowledge actually leaves
Because it describes the current owners with perfect accuracy

questions.explanation: It is predictive because it models a hypothetical future departure to forecast resulting legacy/off-boarding risk before knowledge leaves. D is descriptive, not predictive; A and B forecast unrelated quantities. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.22-23)

questions.stem: What is the recommended response when off-boarding simulation shows a region would turn red if a key author left?

options.text: Immediately delete the at-risk region
Increase that author's workload to retain knowledge
Stop committing to that region entirely
Pair, document, or redistribute ownership before that author departs

questions.explanation: The response is to pair, document, or redistribute ownership before the author leaves, reducing bus-factor risk. A, B and C are harmful or counterproductive. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.22-23)

questions.stem: A team wants to know its 'bus factor' exposure. Which CodeScene capability directly addresses this and how?

options.text: Change coupling, by measuring duplicated lines
Complexity trend, by listing all current owners
Off-boarding simulation, by modelling specific developers leaving and showing which code would become legacy
The hotspot map, by counting violations per file

questions.explanation: Off-boarding simulation models specific departures and shows resulting legacy/off-boarding risk — directly the bus-factor question. D, A and B pair the wrong capability with the wrong mechanism. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.22-23)

questions.stem: Which best summarises the difference between 'how much debt' and 'where to start'?

options.text: 'How much' is the hotspot map; 'where to start' is the debt total
Both are answered identically by static analysis
Neither can be answered by any tool
'How much' is the snapshot's debt total; 'where to start' is the prioritisation the hotspot map provides

questions.explanation: 'How much' is the snapshot's total (e.g. 11% / 683 man-days); 'where to start' is the hotspot-map prioritisation the deck advocates. A inverts the two; B and C are false. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.8, p.12)

questions.stem: Which scenario correctly distinguishes 'principal' from 'cost' in the deck's terms?

options.text: The daily-changed file has near-zero cost because it is small
A 30k-LOC file untouched for years has high principal but near-zero cost; a 500-LOC file changed daily has lower principal but higher cost
Both files have identical cost because cost equals principal
The untouched file has the higher cost because cost equals complexity

questions.explanation: Cost is principal x interest: the untouched 30k file has high principal but ~zero interest (cost), while the small daily-changed file has higher cost from frequent interest. C and D equate cost with principal/complexity; A ignores its high change frequency. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.12)

questions.stem: What is the most accurate reading of 'static analysis will never tell you if excess complexity actually matters'?

options.text: Static tools see complexity but not how often you must work with it, so they cannot judge its real cost
Static tools cannot measure complexity at all
Static tools measure change frequency but not complexity
Static tools always over-state which complexity matters

questions.explanation: Static tools see complexity but not change frequency (how often you work with the code), so they can't judge real cost. B and C misstate what static tools measure; D inverts the limitation. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.10)

questions.stem: Which is the correct order of CodeScene's pipeline outputs after the ML/pattern-detector stage?

options.text: Compiled binaries and test reports
Visualizations, priorities, and predictive analytics
Raw source code and git logs
A single SonarQube violation count

questions.explanation: After the ML/pattern-detector stage, the pipeline outputs visualizations, priorities, and predictive analytics. C is the input; D is the snapshot; A is unrelated build output. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.11)

questions.stem: How does the deck's argument reframe the purpose of measuring debt at all?

options.text: To prove the codebase is unfixable
To replace developers with automated tooling
To prioritise the highest-ROI repayment, not to produce an impressive but unactionable total
To generate the largest possible debt figure for reporting

questions.explanation: The purpose of measurement is highest-ROI prioritisation, not an impressive unactionable total. D, A and B misstate the goal the conclusion sets out. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.25)

questions.stem: Why does the guide say a hotspot is 'the rational first target' rather than the largest or oldest file?

options.text: Because tooling can only refactor hotspots
Because the hotspot combines complexity and frequent change, so it has the highest interest and the most expensive future changes
Because the largest file always has the most faults
Because the oldest file is always the most complex

questions.explanation: A hotspot is the rational first target because complexity x frequent change yields the highest interest and most expensive future changes. C and D rely on size/age alone; A is false. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.12)

questions.stem: In what sense does the deck go 'beyond the code' when discussing legacy code?

options.text: It looks at code outside the repository's main branch
It examines code beyond the function level
It treats debt as also social — legacy, knowledge loss, off-boarding risk — not only a property of the code text
It analyses code written in languages beyond Java

questions.explanation: 'Beyond the code' means treating debt as social (legacy, knowledge loss, off-boarding), not just a property of the code text. D, A and B misread 'beyond the code' literally. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.20)

questions.stem: What is the best description of how the deck uses the word 'movie' as a methodological claim (not literally)?

options.text: That analysing change over time (the VCS history) reveals what a single still frame (snapshot) cannot
That CodeScene literally renders the codebase as a video file
That developers should watch recordings of each other coding
That the analysis must run for the length of a feature film

questions.explanation: 'Movie' is a methodological metaphor: analysing change over time reveals what a single snapshot frame cannot. B, C and D take the metaphor literally. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.9)

questions.stem: Considering the whole deck, which single sentence best captures its thesis?

options.text: Rewrite any file whose complexity exceeds a fixed threshold
Treat technical debt as a purely aesthetic, developer-only concern
Mine the VCS to prioritise the debt that actually costs you (hotspots), but let human expertise make the final call for the highest ROI
Count all static violations and fix them in order of severity

questions.explanation: The deck's thesis: use VCS-mined behavioural analysis to prioritise the debt that actually costs interest (hotspots), with human expertise making the final, highest-ROI call. D and A revert to the snapshot/threshold view; B denies debt's business reality. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.10-25)

questions.stem: Why does the guide say that 'where the debt sits' matters more than 'how much' there is?

options.text: Because the total amount of debt cannot be measured
Because all locations charge identical interest
Because only the largest files ever charge interest
Because debt in frequently-changed code charges high interest, while the same amount in untouched code is dormant

questions.explanation: Location matters more than amount because debt in frequently-changed code charges high interest while the same amount in untouched code is dormant. A, B and C deny the location-dependent interest. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.2, p.12)

questions.stem: A developer says 'our reddest hotspot must be rewritten entirely this sprint.' Which combined critique does the deck support?

options.text: Refactor a different, untouched file instead to be safe
Use X-Ray to scope to the worst functions, read its complexity trend, and check ownership before committing to any rewrite
Rewrite immediately, because redness alone justifies a full rewrite
Ignore the hotspot, because static analysis is more reliable

questions.explanation: The deck supports scoping via X-Ray, reading the trend, and checking ownership before any rewrite — not a sprint-long blind rewrite. C is the boil-the-ocean mistake; D contradicts the behavioural argument; A refactors dormant debt. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.15-23)

questions.stem: Which statement about JIRA's role in CodeScene's pipeline is correct?

options.text: JIRA (project-management data) is an input alongside source code and git history
JIRA is the final output of the pipeline
JIRA replaces the version-control system as the sole data source
JIRA is not used anywhere in the pipeline

questions.explanation: JIRA (project-management tools) is an input alongside source code and git history. B, C and D mis-place JIRA in the pipeline. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.11)

questions.stem: Why does the guide describe the divergence of 'complexity rising vs LOC flat' as 'complexity outpacing size'?

options.text: Because LOC and complexity always move together
Because the file is gaining lines faster than complexity
Because complexity grows even without the file growing, so each line is getting harder — the signature of accumulating principal
Because the file is shrinking while complexity falls

questions.explanation: Complexity outpacing size means complexity grows even though the file barely grows, so each line gets harder — accumulating principal. D, A and B describe non-divergent or inverted trends. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.15)

questions.stem: What role does 'human expertise' play once CodeScene has produced its priorities?

options.text: It is bypassed entirely in favour of the tool's ranking
It only writes documentation and never touches prioritisation
It overrides the data by always picking the least-red file
It makes the final repayment decisions, using the data as support, to maximise ROI

questions.explanation: Human expertise makes the final decisions with the data as support, maximising ROI. A bypasses the human; B limits their role wrongly; C is an arbitrary inversion. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.25)

questions.stem: Which property does NOT define a hotspot?

options.text: High change frequency
Being complicated code you work with often
The number of comments in the file
High code complexity

questions.explanation: A hotspot is defined by high complexity and high change frequency ('complicated code you work with often'); comment count is not part of the definition. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.12)

questions.stem: Which of these is NOT one of the conclusion's messages?

options.text: Technical debt is real and language-independent
The VCS holds a huge amount of useful information
Data should support developers' judgement for the highest ROI
Tools should replace human expertise entirely

questions.explanation: The conclusion says rely on human expertise (tools support, not replace), so 'tools should replace human expertise' is NOT a conclusion message. The other three are genuine conclusion points. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.25)

questions.stem: Which of these is NOT a dimension that behavioural analysis adds over static analysis?

options.text: The compiler's optimization level
The time aspect (evolution over VCS history)
Organization & people (who works on the code)
Prioritisation based on how the organisation works with the code

questions.explanation: Behavioural analysis adds time, organization & people, and organisation-based prioritisation; the compiler's optimization level is not one of them. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.10-11)

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT a cause of technical debt the guide lists?

options.text: Ignoring complexity under schedule pressure
Knowledge loss from staff turnover
Adopting continuous integration
Software evolution itself (Lehman's laws)

questions.explanation: The listed causes include software evolution, ignoring complexity, age/accretion, knowledge loss, and hidden coupling; adopting CI is a good practice, not a cause of debt. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.3-23)

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT part of CodeScene's analysis pipeline inputs?

options.text: Source code
Version-control data (git)
Project-management tools (e.g. JIRA)
The production server's runtime logs

questions.explanation: The pipeline inputs are source code, version-control data, and project-management tools; production runtime logs are not an input. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.11)

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT a 'much more in CodeScene' capability listed on slide 24?

options.text: Automatic generation of the user manual
Change coupling
Proactive warnings
Branch analyses

questions.explanation: Slide 24 lists change coupling, microservices analysis, proactive warnings, retrospectives, delivery performance, and branch analyses; automatic user-manual generation is not among them. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.24)

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT a Lehman law the deck uses?

options.text: (Both Continuing Change and Increasing Complexity are used)
Decreasing Complexity
Continuing Change
Increasing Complexity

questions.explanation: The deck uses Continuing Change and Increasing Complexity; 'Decreasing Complexity' is not a Lehman law and contradicts Increasing Complexity. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.3)

questions.stem: How does the guide tie change coupling to the JHotDraw change exercises specifically?

options.text: It says change coupling measures JHotDraw's runtime speed
It says change coupling reveals which JHotDraw classes historically co-change, complementing the static dependency-graph search in concept location and impact analysis
It says change coupling renames JHotDraw's GoF pattern classes
It says change coupling replaces JHotDraw's need for design patterns

questions.explanation: For JHotDraw, change coupling reveals historically co-changing classes, complementing the static dependency-graph search of concept location and impact analysis. C, D and A misstate change coupling's role. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.19)

questions.stem: Why does the guide say JHotDraw illustrates the 'preventive half' of the debt story?

options.text: Because it has no version-control history to analyse
Because it deliberately uses no design patterns
Because its clean, pattern-rich design avoids borrowing (keeps principal low), the complement to this lecture's diagnose-and-repay focus
Because it shows how to accumulate the most debt fastest

questions.explanation: JHotDraw illustrates prevention: clean, pattern-rich design avoids borrowing (low principal), complementing the diagnose/repay focus of L11. D, A and B invert JHotDraw's exemplary role. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.13)

questions.stem: Which combination correctly maps the worked-example steps to change-process phases per the guide?

options.text: None of the steps map to any change-process phase
Hotspot mapping + change coupling enrich Concept Location & Impact Analysis; X-Ray + refactoring scope Prefactoring/Postfactoring; hotspots guide where Verification is hardest
Hotspot mapping is Verification; refactoring is Concept Location; X-Ray is Impact Analysis
All steps map only to Verification

questions.explanation: The guide maps hotspot mapping/change coupling to Concept Location & Impact Analysis, X-Ray/refactoring to Prefactoring/Postfactoring, and hotspots to where Verification must be hardest. C scrambles the mapping; D and A deny it. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.13-23)

questions.stem: What does the guide mean by saying behavioural metrics are the 'quantitative, prioritised' version of code smells?

options.text: Behavioural metrics are vaguer than smells
Smells already rank problems, making behavioural metrics redundant
Smells qualitatively flag problems; hotspots and change coupling quantify and rank them so you know which to fix first
Smells and behavioural metrics measure completely unrelated things

questions.explanation: Code smells qualitatively flag issues; behavioural metrics quantify and prioritise them. D denies the link; A inverts precision; B wrongly says smells already rank. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.19, p.24)

questions.stem: Which best explains why the deck's whole methodology hinges on the change-frequency (interest) dimension?

options.text: Because change frequency is the only thing a VCS records
Because complexity cannot be measured at all in practice
Because change frequency replaces the need to read code
Because without it you cannot tell which complexity actually costs you, and fault research shows change frequency predicts defects better than size

questions.explanation: The methodology hinges on change frequency because it reveals which complexity costs you, and fault research shows it predicts defects better than size. A, B and C give false or weak reasons. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.10-14)

questions.stem: What is the correct interpretation of 'complicated code that you have to work with often' as a hotspot definition?

options.text: It conjoins high complexity (complicated) with high change frequency (work with often)
It means code that is merely large, regardless of change
It means code that is rarely touched but very complex
It means simple code touched frequently

questions.explanation: The definition conjoins complexity ('complicated') with change frequency ('work with often') — the two hotspot axes. B drops change frequency; C and D drop one axis each. (BeyondTechnicalDebt p.12)
